one_line_takeaway: ELIQ shows that MLLM-derived, automatically-constructed positive/negative comparisons can replace human MOS labels for AI-generated-image quality and prompt-alignment scoring, leading the label-free field on AIGC benchmarks while still lagging supervised methods; it is an IQA/evaluation paper with no security dimension despite its feed cluster label.
key_finding: Among label-free / opinion-unaware methods, ELIQ attains the best reported visual-quality SRCC on all three AIGC benchmarks without any target-domain MOS fine-tuning: 0.801 (AGIQA-3K), 0.767 (AIGCIQA2023), and 0.786 (AIGIQA-20K), substantially above prior label-free baselines such as DUBMA (0.684/0.671/0.695) and QualiCLIP (0.667/0.646/0.679). It also transfers to UGC in the label-free setting (SRCC 0.818 KonIQ-10k, 0.842 SPAQ) without architectural change, though there its margin over QualiCLIP (0.817/0.841) is essentially negligible. For prompt-image alignment (label-free), ELIQ reaches SRCC 0.717 (AGIQA-3K) and 0.712 (AIGCIQA2023), well above the strongest label-free baseline PickScore (0.593/0.626). Ablations confirm conventional and AI-specific negatives are complementary (using both gives the best SRCC 0.801 visual / 0.717 alignment vs 0.796 / 0.782 for either alone) and that all three degradation aspects (technical+aesthetic+alignment) together outperform any subset.
core_contribution: ELIQ is a label-free framework for no-reference image quality assessment (IQA) of AI-generated images that dispenses with human Mean Opinion Score (MOS) supervision. Its central idea is to replace absolute MOS labels (which the authors argue become miscalibrated as generative models rapidly improve, a 'perceptual drift') with automatically constructed relative comparisons. The pipeline (1) generates 1,200 high-quality positive images from 400 curated prompts using three T2I models, then synthesizes aspect-specific negatives via two degradation families (conventional and AI-specific), each along three dimensions: technical, aesthetic, and alignment quality; (2) instruction-tunes a pretrained MLLM (Qwen3-VL-8B-Instruct) into a 'quality-aware critic' that outputs discrete low/high judgments per aspect; and (3) freezes that backbone and trains a lightweight scoring head consisting of a gated visual-alignment fusion module plus a Quality Query Transformer (QQT) with two learnable query tokens, supervised only by margin-based pairwise ranking losses, to predict two continuous scores (visual quality and prompt-image alignment) from a single image at inference.
threat_model: Not applicable. This is not a security paper. There is no adversary, attack, or threat model. Despite the misleading feed cluster label 'GAN Attacks & Forgery,' the paper concerns perceptual quality/aesthetics/prompt-alignment measurement for generative-model outputs. The word 'attack' does not appear; 'degradations' refer to benign synthetic distortions (JPEG compression, Gaussian noise, prompt shuffling, early-diffusion-step decoding) used purely to create training negatives, not adversarial perturbations. No fabricated threat frame should be imposed.
limitations: (1) Even the best label-free visual-quality numbers trail fully supervised methods by a wide margin (e.g., MA-AGIQA supervised 0.893 vs ELIQ label-free 0.801 on AGIQA-3K); the headline 'competitive with supervised' claim applies mainly to the weak-supervised variant that still consumes 20-30% MOS labels. (2) Training data is small and era-specific: only 1,200 positives from a fixed 2025-era set of T2I models and 400 prompts, so the very 'perceptual drift' the paper targets could re-limit it as models evolve, and the periodic 're-generation' of supervision is asserted but not empirically demonstrated over time. (3) On UGC label-free benchmarks the improvement over QualiCLIP is within ~0.001 SRCC, so the AIGC-to-UGC generalization claim is weak on the numbers. (4) Optional MOS-range calibration reintroduces a small labeled validation dependency. (5) Four of the design-choice ablations (instruction tuning necessity, gated visual representation, QQT role, ranking objective) are deferred entirely to the Appendix and thus unverifiable from the main text. (6) No statistical significance testing or variance across runs is reported.

datasets_used: Self-constructed training set: 400 GPT-5-generated prompts across 7 categories, 1,200 positive images from Qwen-Image + FLUX.1-dev + Stable Diffusion 3.5-Large
quantitative_results: Visual quality label-free SRCC: AGIQA-3K 0.801, AIGCIQA2023 0.767, AIGIQA-20K 0.786, KonIQ-10k 0.818, SPAQ 0.842
Visual quality label-free PLCC: AGIQA-3K 0.827, AIGCIQA2023 0.781, AIGIQA-20K 0.803, KonIQ-10k 0.831, SPAQ 0.851
Visual quality weak-supervised SRCC (20% AIGC / 30% UGC MOS): AGIQA-3K 0.876, AIGCIQA2023 0.837, AIGIQA-20K 0.856, KonIQ-10k 0.912, SPAQ 0.915
Alignment quality label-free SRCC: AGIQA-3K 0.717, AIGCIQA2023 0.712 (PLCC 0.730 / 0.711)
Alignment quality weak-supervised SRCC: AGIQA-3K 0.789, AIGCIQA2023 0.804
Ablation Table 4 (degradation family, visual SRCC/alignment SRCC): Conv-only 0.796/0.713, AI-spec-only 0.782/0.702, both 0.801/0.717

Ablation Table 5 (aspects, full Tec+Aes+Ali): visual SRCC 0.801/PLCC 0.827, alignment SRCC 0.717/PLCC 0.730; single-aspect best visual is Tec-only 0.773, single-aspect best alignment is Ali-only 0.712
Backbone: Qwen3-VL-8B-Instruct; metrics: Spearman SRCC and Pearson PLCC
baselines_compared: Supervised: BRISQUE, HyperIQA, MANIQA, DBCNN, MUSIQ, StairIQA, Q-Align, MA-AGIQA (best supervised, e.g. AGIQA-3K SRCC 0.893)
Weak-supervised: CONTRIQUE, Re-IQA, CLIP-IQA+, ARNIQA, GRepQ-D
Label-free visual: NIQE, ILNIQE, CLIP-IQA, MDFS, QualiCLIP, GRepQ-Z, DUBMA
Alignment: CLIP, CLIP-IQA+, ImageReward, HPSv1, PickScore, StairReward, IPCE
